questions.description: Claire regularly drinks "low fat soy cappuccinos" at Donal's Place, her… Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Claire regularly drinks "low fat soy cappuccinos" at Donal's Place, her local coffee shop. On 29th July when she goes in and orders her drink, she is told by Donal that this is the millionth cup of coffee sold there. Donal asks whether Claire, as their "millionth customer", is willing to do some publicity work for the shop, in return for a "lifelong supply of free coffee" in the shop. Claire agrees, and poses for lots of photographs, generating lots of publicity for the coffee shop. She continues to receive free "low fat soy cappuccinos" every day for the next few months. However, on 2nd January when she goes in and orders her usual drink, she is told that from now on she will only be allowed regular coffees, and that she will be limited to one coffee a week. Claire believes this is a breach of contract, but Donal argues that that the reference to a lifelong supply of coffee was a mere "advertising puff' which Claire should not have believed. He also says that even if it was an offer, it was simply a gratuitous gift and not part of any binding contract. Claire is so annoyed with the entire situation that she decides not to drink coffee in Donal's Place any more, but she believes she had a contract with Donal and wants to bring an action against Donal for breach of contract. On hearing Claire's story, her friend Ben phones her to ask whether she would be interested in acquiring his new state of the art coffee machine. He explains that he received the coffee machine as a gift at Christmas but does not drink coffee, and if she doesn't take it he ''would just give it away to charity". Claire is delighted and immediately agrees to take the coffee machine. When she collects the coffee machine the next day she is so happy with it that she promises to give Ben €200 for it when she gets her wages on Wednesday. Ben 'is delighted by this and in the knowledge that he will get €200 on Wednesday he goes out for an expensive dinner that night. 'However, when Claire is paid her wages on Wednesday she does not give Ben any money; 'Ben is now threatening to sue Claire for the €200. Advise Claire on her legal position vis à vis (a) Donal and (b) Ben.

questions.description: The Luxury Ireland Hotel is a premier hotel in Dublin. Negligenc, Exclusion Clause.
questions.text: The Luxury Ireland Hotel is a premier hotel in Dublin. The hotel has a leisure centre with a swimming pool, and has various services for its clients, including the provision of dry cleaning as part of the cost of the room. Tom stays in the hotel in January 2013. When he arrives at reception he completes and signs, but does not read, a registration form. The form includes the following clause: "6. All members and guests use the leisure facilities and swimming pool at their own risk and the club does not accept responsibility for any harm or injury to a guest howsoever caused." Tom uses the swimming pool that evening. However, due to the negligence of the hotel staff the water has been treated with too high a level of chlorine. This causes Tom's eyes to sting and he develops a painful skin rash. He has to visit his doctor and take several days off work. Tom demands compensation for his injuries, the manager of the hotel claims that clause 6 means that the hotel is not liable for Tom's injuries. Niamh is also staying in the Luxury Ireland Hotel in January 2013. She decides to make use of the hotel's dry cleaning service to get her suit cleaned, as the service is included in the cost of the room. She leaves her suit in a plastic bag labelled "Dry Cleaning" to be taken to be cleaned. In fact, the hotel has sub-contracted with a third party, Clean Clothes Ltd., to do all its dry cleaning; and all the clothes from the hotel are taken there on a daily basis. When Niamh's suit is returned there is a large red stain on the back of the jacket and the trousers are ripped. Niamh is furious, but when she threatens to bring an action against the Luxury Ireland Hotel and Clean Clothes Ltd, the manager of the hotel points to an exclusion clause printed on the "Dry Cleaning" plastic bag. It states: "Luxury Ireland Hotel and its employees and agents are not liable in tort or contract for any damage caused to clothes during the dry cleaning process, howsoever caused." (a) Advise Tom on whether clause 6 will prevent him from bringing an action against Luxury Ireland Hotel. (b) Advise Niamh on whether the exclusion clause on the dry cleaning bag will prevent her from bringing an action against Luxury Ireland Hotel. (c) Advise Niamh on whether the exclusion clause on the dry cleaning bag will prevent her from bringing an action against Clean Clothes Ltd.

questions.description: Louise runs a transport company, with a fleet of 150 lorries. Frustrat, Implied Term.
questions.text: Louise runs a transport company, with a fleet of 150 lorries. ID September 2012 Louise enters into a contract with Alan to transport toys from a warehouse in Cork to toy shops across Ireland over the Christmas period, 15th November to 24th December. The total contract price is €20,000, with €5,000 to be paid in advance on 10th November and the rest due on 5th January. Alan pays €5,000 on 1Oth November. However on 12th November there is an accidental fire in the toy warehouse and the warehouse and all its contents are destroyed. Alan claims that the contract with Louise is frustrated, and that he is entitled to the return of the €5,000 paid. Louise claims that the lorries are still available to Alan for the entire Christmas period and that she should be able to keep the €5,000 paid and claim the remaining €15,000 due. In 2007, Louise enters into a ten year transport contract with Supermarket Ltd, a major Irish supermarket chain. Louise agrees to transport and deliver goods from Supermarket Ltd.'s warehouses to its supermarkets for a period of ten years, at a fixed price of €2 million per year. However, subsequent to this agreement, political crises in the Middle East and unexpected changes in market conditions result in an increase in the cost of oil and petrol. By 2013 petrol prices in Ireland have increased to such an extent that Louise is performing the contract with Supermarket Ltd at a loss. Louise wants to know if she has any grounds on which she can terminate the transport contract with Supermarket Ltd. In addition, for the last five years Louise has regularly transported furniture around the country for Sean, a furniture manufacturer. They have no written contract. Each time Sean needs furniture delivered he simply contacts Louise and she invariably agrees to transport it for him. This occurs approximately once a month. However, in February 2013 Louise decides that she wants to focus on other aspects of the transport business and that she no longer wants to deliver Sean's furniture. Sean however claims that she cannot simply terminate their "arrangement" without notice. He claims that they have a contract and that there is an implied term that Louise should give him at least six months' notice of her intention to terminate the contract. Louise claims there is no such contract or implied term. Advise Louise on her legal position vis à vis (a) Alan and (b) Supermarket Ltd and (c) Sean.

questions.description: Ann is a world famous Irish actress. Company.
questions.text: Ann is a world famous Irish actress. In October 2012 she agrees with the film company Irish Movies Ltd to play the lead role in a science fiction film called "Alien Attack". She is to receive a payment of €1 million for making the film. Even before Ann signs the contract Irish Movies Ltd spends €500,000 on producing the movie set. Following on from Ann's signature of the contract the company spends €200,000 on special effects in which Ann's image is manipulated by digital technology. However, shortly after signing the contract, Ann informs Irish Movies Ltd that she will no longer be appearing in the film "Alien Attack". She will instead by starring in a rival film "Vampire Attack''. It is widely reported that her reason for doing so was the prospect of greater future earnings from the Vampire Attack franchise, as she was offered €2 million and 1% of box office takings to appear in that film. Irish Movies Ltd decides that without Ann there is no point going ahead with the film, and therefore cancels production. They predict that if the film had gone ahead with Ann as planned "that they would have made profits of€5 million. They now wish to sue Ann for their lost profits and for their wasted expenditure. They also wish to recover from Ann the additional fee that she received for appearing in "Vampire Attack", as they claim she only obtained this extra profit as a result of a "deliberate and cynical breach" of her original contract. However, Ann says that Irish Movies Ltd could easily have hired another actress to replace her, and that the real reason they cancelled production was that the film script for "Alien Attack" was so terrible that they would not have made any profit from the film had it gone ahead. Advise Irish Movies Ltd.

questions.description: Discuss the impact of EU directives and current harmonisation initiatives on Irish contract law.
questions.text: Discuss the impact of EU directives and current harmonisation initiatives on Irish contract law.

questions.description: Answer (a) [and]{.underline} (b) (a) Outline the extent to which the courts take into account background information…
questions.text: Answer (a) [and]{.underline} (b) (a) Outline the extent to which the courts take into account background information when interpreting contractual documents. [And]{.underline} (b) When will the Irish courts imply a term into a contract? (Please note it is not necessary to discuss the implication of terms by statute or the Constitution in your answer.)

Answer (a) [or]{.underline} (b) a. Outline the duty of utmost good faith in Irish insurance contract law. [Or]{.underline} b. Discuss whether each of the following could be an actionable misrepresentation: (i) a statement of intention; (ii) a statement of opinion; and (iii) silence.

questions.description: Explain the approach of the Irish courts to a situation where a person standing as surety (guarantor) for a loan or…
questions.text: Explain the approach of the Irish courts to a situation where a person standing as surety (guarantor) for a loan or mortgage from a bank claims they acted under the undue influence of a third party.

questions.description: Harry owns an antique watch, a set of antique dishes and some antique… Consideration, Breach Of Contract.
questions.text: Harry owns an antique watch, a set of antique dishes and some antique coins that he decides to sell. On Monday Harry contacts a local jeweller, Brenda, to offer sell to her the antique watch for €150. Although Brenda is not overly interested in the watch, Harry tells her that he won't sell it to anyone else before Thursday to give Brenda time to think about it. However, on Tuesday Harry meets his friend Chloe, who says that she will pay Harry €150 for the watch. Harry is delighted and immediately accepts. That evening Harry emails Brenda via her website to tell her that he has sold the watch to somebody else. Brenda does not check her emails outside working hours and does not see the email. On Wednesday morning on the way to work Brenda decides that she wants to buy the watch and immediately phones Harry to tell him this. When Harry responds that he has already sold the watch, Brenda is furious and says that ''they had a deal" that he would keep his offer open until Thursday. Brenda is threatening to take legal action against Harry. On Tuesday Harry hears that Marie, a friend of one of his work colleagues, is a collector of antique dishes and plates, and that she might be interested in buying his plates. In a brief set of emails Marie promises to pay Harry €70 for the dishes, payment upon delivery, and Harry promises to deliver the dishes the following Saturday. However the next day Harry phones Marie to tell her he has changed his mind and no longer wants to sell the dishes. He claims that as Marie has not yet performed ''her part of the deal" or provided any consideration, he is entitled to do so. Marie wants to sue Harry for breach of contract. The next weekend Harry puts the antique coins up for auction without any reserve price. At the beginning of the auction the auctioneer states that all sales are ''without reserve unless otherwise stated". Deirdre bids €50 for the coins, and seems to be the only bidder interested in them. Harry isn't happy with this as he thinks the coins are worth at least €200. He instructs the auctioneer to withdraw the coins from the auction, and the auctioneer does this. Deirdre is threatening to sue both Harry and the auctioneer. Advise Harry.

questions.description: Sarah is a vet and owns her own veterinary surgery. Penalty Clause.
questions.text: Sarah is a vet and owns her own veterinary surgery. Last March she decided to expand her business and she hired Jeff to build a fully completed extension on to the surgery. The contract between Sarah and Jeff clearly stated that the work was to be finished by June 30th, and a penalty clause stated if the work was not completed by this date that Jeff was liable to pay €500 per day until the extension was fully complete. In April, Jeff entered into a sub­contract with Mark, a local plumber who agreed to carry out all the plumbing work on the extension by June 15th, at a fixed price of €5,000. At the time of contracting, Jeff emphasised that it was essential that this work be done on time, saying that it was going to cost him "a fortune" if he was late in completing the work for Sarah. At the start of June Mark contacted Jeff to say that the work was taking longer than he had anticipated. He said that he was going to need an extra payment of €2,000 to enable him to hire extra workers so he could finish the job on time. Jeff reluctantly paid the money and Mark managed to finish on time. However, once the work was done Jeff wrote Mark a letter demanding the return of the additional €2,000 paid, saying that it was not part of the original agreed price and that Mark had "done nothing extra for the cash". In addition, one of Sarah's clients, Janice, a local farmer, owes Sarah €2,000 in veterinary fees. In May Janice approached Sarah and said that she couldn't afford to pay her the full amount due, but that she'd pay her €800. Janice said Sarah could accept that "or get nothing'', so Sarah reluctantly accepted the lesser amount Sarah now regrets this decision, and wants to know if she can sue Janice for the difference of €1,200. Advise Jeff and Sarah vis-à-vis their respective rights against Mark and Janice.

questions.description: In April Ted sells his ice cream van, complete with ice cream making… Advise her if she can return the ice cream van to…
questions.text: In April Ted sells his ice cream van, complete with ice cream making equipment, to Carla. During negotiations Ted says that last summer he made €7,000 selling ice cream and that next summer is due to be hot so Carla should make a profit of €9,000. He offers to show Carla the accounts but Carla says she's "not great with numbers" and that she will ''take his word for it". However, a month after purchasing the van Carla discovers from a friend in the ice cream trade that Ted hadn't used his ice cream van at all the previous summer, so had made no profit at all. Furthermore her friend tells her that it's very unlikely that she will make a profit of €9,000 over the summer, as the average summer profit for an ice cream van is €5,000. Carla is furious and regrets buying the ice cream van. Late last May Carla asked you to advise her if she can return the ice cream van to Ted and get her money back. After her experience with the ice cream van, Carla decides that the best way to make money over the summer is to set up a surfing school in Cork. She is a surfing expert and has designed a new type of surf board especially for beginners. Leonard is shown the design and in an email is asked by Carla to manufacture 20 copies of the board for €2,000. Leonard replies by email, stating that the surfboards will be "slightly smaller than discussed" and is he "happy to do the job for €200". Carla immediately phones Leonard and asks him to confirm the details in the email. Leonard is on his way to a meeting and replies ''I'm sure the details are correct'' without checking. Carla immediately emails an acceptance agreeing to purchase the surf boards at a total price of €200. Leonard is now refusing to manufacture the surf boards for €200. Carla wants to know if she has a binding contract with Leonard. Advise Carla.

questions.description: Aoife is the manager of a construction site. Advise Aoife on her legal position in relation to both Tom and Sam in…
questions.text: Aoife is the manager of a construction site. She needs a large crane and a digger for the site. In January she enters into a contract to hire an ''XL Super Crane" from Tom for one year. Clause 8 of this contract states: ''It is a term of this contract that the XL Super Crane will be of satisfactory quality for the duration of the hire period." One morning in March, the XL Super Crane suddenly stops working. When Aoife phones Tom to complain, he says that it is a minor problem with the engine and that he will need to take the crane away for one week to repair it. He says that he will waive the hire fee for that week. The crane is repaired, but when Tom returns it to the site one week later Aoife refuses to accept it. She says that Tom is in breach of Clause 8 of the contract and that she is entitled to terminate the hire contract. However, Tom suspects that Aoife simply wants out of the contract as the market for plant hire has fallen and she may have found a cheaper crane elsewhere. Aoife purchases a digger for the construction site from Sam. The written contract of sale is brief and makes no mention of the quality of the digger. However, after one day the digger breaks down and won't work. Aoife wants to return the digger to Sam and get her money back. However, Sam says that he gave no warranty or guarantee about the quality of the digger and that she is "stuck with it now''. Advise Aoife on her legal position in relation to both Tom and Sam in relation to the defective equipment.

Answer (a) or (b). (a) Discuss the need to reform the law on the capacity of a minor to enter into a binding contract. [Or]{.underline} (b) "Certain kinds of contract can only be enforced if evidenced in writing." Discuss.

questions.description: Briefly discuss (a) the principles governing remoteness of damage in Irish… Damages, Penalty Clause.
questions.text: Briefly discuss (a) the principles governing remoteness of damage in Irish contract law [and]{.underline} (b) the difference between a liquidated damages clause and a penalty clause.

questions.description: Which contractual principles are used by the judiciary to protect persons who provide guarantees and securities to…
questions.text: Which contractual principles are used by the judiciary to protect persons who provide guarantees and securities to financial institutions that advance monies to close friends and family members of the guarantor? Cite recent Irish case law in your answer.

questions.description: Answer (a) or (b) (a) "An agent has the power to bind their principal where they have "authority'' to act on the…
questions.text: Answer (a) or (b) (a) "An agent has the power to bind their principal where they have "authority'' to act on the principal's behalf and a distinction is drawn between the various types of authority." Outline how agency operates as an exception to the privity rule and the different types of authority which an agent may have to act on the principal's behalf. [Or]{.underline} (b) Provide a critical assessment of the Law Reform Commission's Report on Privity of Contract (2008).
